posts.title: What ONE Champions Are Watching in African Development Financing
posts.excerpt: A short reflection on where advocacy and public financing intersect right now for youth-focused development work across the continent.
posts.body:
It was an incredible experience joining other vibrant young Africans in Kigali, Rwanda, as the first Pan-African Cohort of the ONE Campaign's ONE Champions Program in Africa, where we engaged in an intensive training and induction activities. We were guided by expert trainers and inspiring speakers who lead us through topics around Africa’s current challenges and development opportunities, with a core focus on development financing and discussions around how domestic resource mobilization can grow the continent.

As someone who has always championed equitable access to economic opportunities as a foundation for dignified lives, the insights gained on development financing and resource mobilization have profoundly impacted my perspective around the untapped potential within Africa, and how we can pave the way for a prosperous and self-reliant continent by effectively harnessing and utilizing our resources. I am excited to collaborate with my fellow ONE Champions as we work together towards an Africa where everyone has the opportunity to live a dignified life

By: Abdulazeez Musa, Founder, YouthXcel Africa

Every July, the same conversation resurfaces around the world: what skills will young people need next. This year's answer, set by UNESCO-UNEVOC for World Youth Skills Day, is “skills for a shared future,” a call for technical ability paired with digital fluency, environmental literacy, and the kind of emotional intelligence no algorithm can replicate. It is a good answer. It is also, on its own, an incomplete one. Because increasingly, across Nigeria and the wider continent, the young people struggling to move forward are not the ones without skills. They are the ones who have built skills and still cannot find where those skills belong.

The numbers tell part of this story, though not the part most people expect. Global youth unemployment has actually fallen to its lowest level in fifteen years, according to the International Labour Organization. And yet hundreds of millions of young people, an estimated 450 million by the United Nations' own count, remain economically disengaged, not because the labour market rejected them outright, but because the pathway toward it was never made visible to them in the first place. A falling unemployment rate sitting alongside a still-swollen population of disengaged youth is not a contradiction. It is evidence that measuring skill acquisition alone no longer tells us who is actually moving forward and who has simply been left standing at the edge of it.

Anyone who has spent real time inside youth programming on this continent has watched this play out at close range. A young graduate finishes a coding bootcamp and has nowhere obvious to apply it. A brilliant final-year student misses a fully funded fellowship, not for lack of qualification, but because she heard about it two days before the deadline, from a friend, on WhatsApp. A community of capable young people sits three clicks away from opportunities that could change the direction of their lives, and those three clicks might as well be three hundred kilometres, because no one ever handed them the map. For a large share of them, it nearly is: adults in rural parts of Sub-Saharan Africa are close to fifty percent less likely to use mobile internet than their counterparts in cities, according to GSMA, which means the very tools people rely on to hear about opportunity are often the ones least available to those who need them most. Skills development has multiplied steadily across the region, through vocational programmes, digital academies, and a growing wave of green-skilling pilots. What has not multiplied at the same pace is the layer of guidance that helps a young person actually find, understand, and reach the opportunities meant for them.

The imbalance runs deepest among those already displaced by conflict or crisis, the group most easily left out of any conversation about opportunity. Refugees currently have access to higher education at a rate of about nine percent, compared with a global average of forty-two percent among their non-displaced peers, according to UNHCR. That gap has little to do with ability. It is what happens when legal status, lost documentation, and unfamiliar systems combine to make even applying for something feel like a locked door. For a young person who has already lost a home, a school, or a sense of place, the ability to navigate what remains available to them is rarely a convenience. It is often the difference between rebuilding a life and simply waiting for one.

I have spent the past several years working alongside young people who carry real skill and real ambition, in communities where circumstance, more than capability, often decided who moved forward and who stayed behind. Some of that understanding comes from personal history, from watching how quickly stability can be taken from a family and how much harder everything becomes once it is. Some of it comes from years spent inside youth policy spaces, including as a Champion with the ONE Campaign and a member of the EU Youth Panel under the Global Youth Mobilization, years that kept surfacing the same pattern in different countries and different rooms. Talented young people were rarely short on ability. They were short on someone, or something, able to point them clearly toward what already existed for them.

This is the argument worth making plainly: opportunity navigation deserves to sit beside technical, digital, and civic skills as a competency in its own right, not underneath them as an afterthought. It is the ability to locate what is available, understand what it requires, prepare for it credibly, and follow through until it converts into something real. A shared future, the kind this year's theme asks the world to build, cannot rest on shared skill alone. It has to rest on shared access to the systems that turn skill into a life. Otherwise the gap simply relocates. Instead of a skills gap, it becomes a knowing gap, quieter, harder to measure, and just as costly.

This is the problem YouthXcel Africa was built to work on. Alongside our work in digital and financial literacy and youth storytelling, we are developing an opportunity navigation model that treats discovery, readiness, and follow-through as skills in their own right, things that can be taught and supported rather than left to chance or proximity to the right network. It is early, deliberate work, built on the belief that the young people who most need opportunity are often the ones furthest from hearing about it, and that closing that distance deserves the same urgency as any training programme.

If this year's theme is genuinely about a shared future, then skills systems, funders, and policymakers need to start treating navigation as infrastructure rather than an accident of who happens to know the right person. That means funding the guidance layer with the same seriousness as the training layer. It means asking not only how many young people were trained, but how many actually found their way to what came next. The young people who most deserve the future everyone keeps describing are often standing right beside its entrance, without anything pointing them toward the door. Naming that gap is the first step toward building the compass this moment keeps asking for.


Sources

UNESCO-UNEVOC, “World Youth Skills Day 2026: Skills for a Shared Future”
United Nations, “World Youth Skills Day” observance page, citing ILO Global Employment Trends for Youth
UNHCR, “Higher Education and Skills”, 15by30 Refugee Higher Education campaign data
GSMA Mobile for Development, “Despite Improvements, Sub-Saharan Africa Has the Widest Usage and Coverage Gaps Worldwide”
posts.title: Skills for a Shared Future Still Need a Compass: Why Opportunity Navigation Is Africa's Missing Youth Competency
posts.excerpt: Increasingly, the young people struggling to move forward are not the ones without skills. They are the ones who have built skills and still cannot find where those skills belong...

posts.title: Half the Population, Two Percent of the Parliament
posts.body:
By: Abdulazeez Musa, Founder, YouthXcel Africa

Half the world's population is under thirty. In national parliaments, the share of lawmakers who are thirty or under sits at 2.8 percent, a figure that has not moved in over a decade, according to the Inter-Parliamentary Union's most recent global count. More than a third of the world's parliamentary chambers currently have no member under thirty at all. The arithmetic is not subtle, and it matters well beyond the symbolism usually attached to it.

It matters specifically for the kind of policy that shapes opportunity. Decisions about how education budgets are allocated, whether technical and vocational training gets funded seriously, and how youth employment schemes are designed tend to be made almost entirely by people two or three decades removed from the labour market they are legislating for. That distance shows up quietly, in programmes built around assumptions about how young people learn, work, and job hunt that stopped being accurate years before the policy was drafted.

The pattern holds firmly across Africa. In South Africa's most recent parliament, young people aged eighteen to thirty four hold roughly eight and a half percent of National Assembly seats, in a country where that age group makes up a far larger share of the population. Only four countries on the continent, Rwanda, Kenya, Uganda, and Morocco, currently reserve any parliamentary seats specifically for youth representatives. Everywhere else, entry depends on the same informal networks and financial barriers that have always favoured older, more established candidates.

It is worth naming the exception alongside the pattern, because the gap is not fixed by nature. Senegal's most recent parliamentary renewal saw youth representation jump from roughly fifteen percent to over thirty percent, a shift the Inter-Parliamentary Union attributes largely to candidate list requirements that obligated parties to include younger candidates in winnable positions. The ceiling moved because a specific rule changed, not because attitudes shifted first and structure followed.

That distinction matters for anyone hoping to close this gap elsewhere. Civic education campaigns and youth engagement forums are worth running, but they tend to produce enthusiasm without changing who actually gets onto a ballot in a winnable position. The interventions that have moved real numbers, in Senegal and in the handful of other countries the IPU points to, involve structural mechanisms, reserved seats, candidate quotas, and aligning the minimum age to run for office with the minimum age to vote, since in a number of countries a citizen can decide an election years before they are legally permitted to stand in one.

An education policy or employment strategy built without a single person in the room who is currently navigating today's job market will carry blind spots that no amount of good intention fixes afterward. Young people are not asking for a symbolic seat at a table that makes decisions about someone else's future. They are the ones who will live longest with the consequences of decisions being made right now, largely without them, and closing that gap is a practical question of better policy, not merely a fairer one.

Sources

Inter-Parliamentary Union, “Youth participation in national parliaments 2025”

Parliamentary Monitoring Group / ParliMeter, “Youth in Parliament 2025”, South Africa

BGIPU reporting on the 2025 IPU youth representation findings
posts.excerpt: An education policy or employment strategy built without a single person in the room who is currently navigating today's job market will carry blind spots that no amount of good intention fixes afterward...

posts.title: Nine in Ten Young Africans Are Trained by a System Nobody Funds
posts.body:
Ask where most young Africans actually learn a trade, and the honest answer surprises people who assume schools are doing most of the work. For the overwhelming majority, it is an apprenticeship, a mentor standing over a workbench, a senior colleague correcting a mistake in real time, an arrangement that no ministry designed, tracks, or funds in any serious way.

Across Western and Central Africa, traditional apprenticeships account for roughly eighty to ninety percent of young people engaged in any form of skills training, according to World Bank research, while formal technical and vocational programmes reach fewer than four percent of secondary students. The ratio runs almost exactly opposite to where public funding and policy attention typically go, with the vast majority of investment aimed at the small formal slice and almost none at the channel actually carrying most young people through.

The same imbalance shows up inside an industry that looks entirely formal from the outside. Roughly a third of Africa's software developers were trained in universities. Almost the same share taught themselves, through online material, practice projects, and communities built with no institution's involvement, according to developer ecosystem research from Google and the International Finance Corporation. Add bootcamps and on the job learning, and informal and semi-formal routes account for a clear majority of how the continent's working developers actually got there.

None of this means the informal route is easy, or that it should be romanticised. It usually means a longer timeline, no credential to show for years of genuine effort, and a real risk of absorbing the wrong information from the wrong source with nobody positioned to correct it. What it does mean is that describing these paths as a fallback for people who could not access the real system gets the direction backwards. For most young people on the continent, this already is the real system. It simply has no name, no budget, and no seat at the policy table.

Picture the shape of it side by side. An apprentice under a master tailor, learning through years of repetition, correction, and the slow accumulation of judgment that no textbook fully captures. A young developer working through free tutorials late at night, building projects nobody assigned, debugging alone until something finally runs. Different settings, the same underlying structure, learning by doing under some form of guidance, entirely outside anything a ministry of education is set up to measure.

The consequence of that blind spot is measurable. Curriculum design, skills funding, and national training strategy get built almost entirely around the formal four percent, while the channel actually carrying most young people receives no investment in quality, no standard way to progress, and no mechanism for identifying and supporting the mentors and master craftspeople who are quietly doing the bulk of the continent's skills development for free.

A more useful shift is not to force apprenticeship into a shape it was never meant to hold, but to fund and study it as what it already is, the primary skilling infrastructure for most of the continent's youth. That could look like modest stipends for master craftspeople who formally take on apprentices, recognised assessments that let self-taught developers prove competency without a degree standing in the way, or simply better data, since at present nobody can say with any precision how many young people are inside this system at a given time.

The language of a skills gap suggests that nothing is happening, that young people are simply waiting for training to arrive. Something is happening, at enormous scale, every single day, in workshops and bedrooms and small community spaces across the continent. It has just never been counted, funded, or taken seriously as the dominant system it already is.


Sources

World Bank, Western and Central Africa Regional Education Strategy

Google and International Finance Corporation, Africa Developer Ecosystem Report

Techpoint Africa and Digital Leap Africa reporting on developer training pathways
posts.excerpt: For most young people on the continent, this already is the real system. It simply has no name, no budget, and no seat at the policy table...
